theme: Every Pixel Has a Job — No Dead Space, No Mute Props

ideas.title: Kill the Mobile Dead Zone
ideas.description: On mobile (390px), the office floor panel ends at roughly 55% of the viewport height, leaving ~300px of empty dark space before the footer — I can see it clearly in the mobile screenshot. The fix: in Office.css, set `.office-wrap` to `min-height: calc(100dvh - var(--header-height) - var(--tabs-height) - 4rem)` and add `height: 100%` to `.office-floor` so the floor stretches to fill the remaining screen. If the floor content is too small to fill that height naturally, use `aspect-ratio: unset` and let it breathe with `padding-bottom: env(safe-area-inset-bottom, 1rem)`. Also add `overflow: hidden` to prevent the ghost scroll. WHY: The dead zone is the first thing a new mobile visitor sees after the office — an unprofessional sea of nothing that makes the app feel unfinished. It's the highest-signal cosmetic bug on the screen and the easiest to fix. It doesn't need a design decision, just a CSS floor expansion.

ideas.title: Vacant Desk Gets a Heartbeat
ideas.description: The bottom-right desk slot currently renders `📋 Vacant` with a `?` ghost silhouette and zero personality — it's dead real estate in the most interactive part of the app. Replace with: in Office.jsx, detect `occ === undefined` (vacant) and render a `desk--hiring` modifier. Inside, swap `📋 Vacant` nameplate for `🔍 Hiring...` with a CSS blink cursor (`@keyframes blink { 50% { opacity: 0 } }`). Add a faint pulsing ring on the monitor element: `.desk--hiring .desk__monitor { animation: hiring-pulse 2.5s ease-in-out infinite; box-shadow: 0 0 0 0 var(--accent); }` with a `@keyframes hiring-pulse` that goes `0% { box-shadow: 0 0 0 0 rgba(168,85,247,0.5) } 70% { box-shadow: 0 0 0 8px transparent }`. Add `data-tooltip='Bloberto is interviewing 🧐'` for hover context. WHY: Every user who sees the vacant slot asks 'what goes here?' — right now the answer is 'nothing'. A hiring pulse says 'the office is alive and growing'. It also sets up a future feature where new hires visually appear at that exact desk, making the animation feel earned.

ideas.title: Task-Type Sticky Notes Float Above Active Desks
ideas.description: The task tag system (Bug 🔴, Ship 🟠, Design 🟣, QA 🔵, Data 🟤, Docs 🟢) is a complete color-coded taxonomy that currently exists ONLY on the Dashboard worker cards — invisible on the office floor. For each working character in Office.jsx, call the existing `getTaskTags(worker.task)` function (import it from App.jsx or extract it to utils). Render a `<div className='desk-sticky'>` positioned absolutely above the desk (`top: -18px; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%)`) showing just the first tag's emoji + a tiny colored dot matching `task-tag--{type}` background. `.desk-sticky { font-size: 0.6rem; background: var(--surface2); border-radius: 4px; padding: 1px 4px; border-left: 2px solid var(--tag-color); white-space: nowrap; }` with `--tag-color` set per tag type. WHY: The office floor is a spatial view of the team — it should encode MORE information than the dashboard, not less. Right now a working character's desk tells you their name and nothing else. One tiny sticky note tells you what kind of work is happening across the whole floor at a glance: three bug tabs and a ship tab means the team is in fire-fighting mode, not building mode. The office floor becomes a task-type heatmap.

ideas.title: 'Find on Floor' — Reverse Navigation from Dashboard Card
ideas.description: Clicking a worker on the office floor already navigates to the Dashboard and focuses their card with a 3s pulse. But the reverse direction is missing: there's no way to go from a Dashboard card BACK to the office floor and see that person's desk. Add a `🏢` button to each `WorkerCard`'s header row (beside the existing `ℹ️` flip button): `<button className='worker-card__floor-btn' onClick={() => { setTab('office'); window.location.hash = 'office'; triggerDeskPulse(worker.id) }} title='Find on floor'>🏢</button>`. In Office.jsx, accept a `pulsingDesk` prop; add `desk--pulsing` class to that desk for 2s: `@keyframes desk-ping { 0% { box-shadow: 0 0 0 0 var(--role-color, #a855f7) } 100% { box-shadow: 0 0 0 16px transparent } }`. Pass `pulsingDesk` down from App.jsx via state. WHY: Users who discover the drill-down (floor → dashboard) naturally want the reverse. More importantly, on mobile where Office and Dashboard are separate tabs, there's currently zero spatial orientation — you can't tell which desk belongs to which card you're reading. This button turns the tab split into a feature rather than a limitation.

ideas.title: Whiteboard Gets Vibe Doodles
ideas.description:
The whiteboard (`.office-whiteboard`) currently shows one vibe-keyed text string from `VIBE_WHITEBOARD`. It has `aria-label`, `role='img'`, and a `--urgent` modifier when `on-fire` — but visually it's just text on a light rectangle. Upgrade to render a small inline SVG doodle alongside the text, keyed to vibe. In Office.jsx, create a `VIBE_WHITEBOARD_SVG` map: `crushing` → a jagged rocket (8-path SVG, ~12 lines); `on-fire` → three stacked flame paths in orange/red; `slow-day` → a coffee mug with a zzz cloud; `after-hours` → a crescent moon + two stars; `in-flow` → a simple flow chart (two rounded rects, arrow). Render as `
{VIBE_WHITEBOARD_SVG[vibe]}{VIBE_WHITEBOARD[vibe]}
`. Each doodle gets a subtle `@keyframes whiteboard-draw` CSS stroke-dashoffset animation (0→1) triggered when vibe changes via `key={vibe}`. WHY: The whiteboard is the largest decorative element on the office floor and the one users stare at while watching Bloberto. Right now it tells you what the vibe is in text — but the office is a visual medium. A rocket scrawled on a whiteboard communicates 'crush mode' better than the words do. It's also the only wall prop that changes state, which makes it feel like evidence that real work is happening.

ideas.title: Activity Log Entry Hover Expands with Worker Snapshot
ideas.description: The activity log entries are dense text rows — worker emoji, icon, message, timestamp — with no interaction beyond hover changing the background. On hover/focus of any `.activity-entry`, expand it to reveal a micro-snapshot of that worker's current state: a `<div className='activity-entry__expand'>` with `max-height: 0; overflow: hidden; transition: max-height 0.2s ease` that animates to `max-height: 48px` on `:hover`. Inside: `{worker.status === 'working' ? '⚡ Currently at {progress}%' : worker.status === 'idle' ? '😴 On break' : worker.status === 'error' ? '❌ In error state' : '⬜ Off duty'}` — looked up from the current `roster` prop already passed to `ActivityLog`. This requires passing `workers` (not just `roster`) down to `ActivityLog`, or lifting the worker lookup to `ActivityLogEntry`. WHY: The activity log shows the PAST. Users naturally want to know 'but what is this person doing RIGHT NOW?' — especially when reading a completion event. The expansion turns a static log into a bridge between history and present state. It also surfaces the worker status system to users who primarily live in the activity log tab.

ideas.title: Bloberto Walks to the Whiteboard to Announce Completions
ideas.description: When a worker's status transitions from `working` → `idle` (the `justShipped` detection already exists in App.jsx's worker diff logic), trigger a 6-second cinematic sequence on the office floor: (1) Bloberto's Character element gets class `char--walking-left` which CSS-animates `left` from 46% to 18% over 1.2s with a bob keyframe; (2) at the 1.3s mark, the whiteboard temporarily overrides its content to `'✅ {workerName} shipped it!'` with a new `celebWhiteboard` state prop passed to Office.jsx; (3) after 3 seconds, the whiteboard gets class `office-whiteboard--celebrate` with a green glow (`box-shadow: 0 0 16px #22c55e`); (4) at 5s, Bloberto walks back right (`char--walking-right`) and whiteboard reverts. All driven by a single `useEffect` on `justShipped` with sequential `setTimeout`s. The walk animations are two `@keyframes` — left-walk and right-walk — each just a `left` transition paired with the existing `bob` animation. WHY: Right now the completion event fires a toast and confetti — both screen-layer effects that float above the scene. But nothing on the OFFICE FLOOR changes. The characters at their desks are oblivious. Bloberto walking over to write the name on the whiteboard makes the office feel like a place with gravity — things that happen in it leave marks. It's the kind of thing users will open the tab specifically to watch.
